critique.summary: The article announces the release of Java 26, detailing its new features, repreviewed features, and deprecations. It highlights improvements in JVM performance, HTTP/3 support, structured concurrency, lazy constants, and enhancements in pattern matching and vector API, along with preparations for future Project Valhalla features.
mainPoints: Java 26 introduces new JVM performance features like ahead-of-time object caching compatible with any garbage collector (JEP 516) and improved G1 garbage collector throughput by reducing synchronization (JEP 522).
The HTTP Client API now supports HTTP/3 (JEP 517), allowing faster and more reliable HTTP communications with fallback mechanisms to older protocols.
Structured Concurrency (JEP 525) is previewed to improve concurrency by enforcing hierarchical task management, cancellation propagation, and better error handling.
Lazy Constants (JEP 526) provide a new way to defer immutable object initialization safely and efficiently, improving startup performance and concurrency safety.

The Vector API (JEP 529) continues incubation to enable efficient SIMD vector computations, and pattern matching enhancements (JEP 530) extend support for primitive types in instanceof and switch constructs.
Java 26 deprecates unsafe mutation of final fields via deep reflection, preparing for stricter enforcement in future releases (JEP 500).
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the features introduced in Java 26, referencing specific JEPs (JDK Enhancement Proposals) that correspond to official Java development efforts. The technical explanations align with publicly available information from OpenJDK and Oracle regarding Java 26 features. The descriptions of JVM improvements, HTTP/3 support, structured concurrency, lazy constants, vector API, and pattern matching enhancements are consistent with official JEP documentation. The article also correctly notes the preview status of several features and the deprecation plans for final field mutation, which are documented in JEP 500. Overall, the claims are factual and well-supported by official sources.
considerations: The article does not provide perspectives or critiques from independent Java experts or the broader developer community regarding the impact or adoption challenges of these new features.
It does not discuss potential backward compatibility issues or migration concerns for existing Java applications upgrading to Java 26.
The preview status of several features means they are subject to change and not finalized, which developers should consider before adopting them in production.
The article assumes familiarity with Java internals and JEPs, which might limit accessibility for less experienced readers.
The performance benefits described, such as those for the G1 GC improvements and Vector API, may vary depending on application workload and hardware.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion reflects on the evolution and current state of Java, highlighting improvements in language features and ecosystem maturity while also addressing cultural and legacy challenges. Some comments express nostalgia and frustration with older Java versions and Android's lag in adopting new Java features.

comments.author: ludovicianul
comments.text: I program in Java for more than 15 years now. I can resonate with people hating the language from it's early days due to the experience with all the enterprisy features and over abstractions. Or confunding Java with the Spring ecosystem. But Java came a long way over the years. It's now what many would call a "modern" language. It's less verbose, has many of the features people find appealing in Scala and Kotlin and it can even compile to native binaries using GraalVM. This made building CLIs in Java feasible. Or lambdas.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a knowledgeable and balanced perspective on Java's evolution, accurately noting recent improvements and practical uses like GraalVM.

comments.author: xeubie
comments.text:
I think astronomers could measure the age of the universe in nano-Valhallas. Every year, it feels 50% closer to completion...

In all seriousness I'm happy with what Mr. Goetz and the team have done. Sealed interfaces (java 17) + exhaustive switch statements (java 21) means we now have union types in java! And instead of jumping on the async/await bandwagon we now have a more general solution that doesn't lead to API duplication (virtual threads). But Valhalla has been a veeery long time coming.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly highlights recent Java language features and their significance, showing a good understanding of the technical advancements.

comments.author: vyskocilm
comments.text:
JEP 504: Remove the Applet API

Glad to see this being removed. Java plugins especially on Linux were awful and required by tons of corporate stuff. Anyone remeber IcedTea Web? A functional and opensource Java plugin and Java Webstart implementation?
comments.analysis: This comment accurately reflects the outdated nature of Java applets and the positive move to remove them, referencing a known open-source alternative.

comments.author: einrealist
comments.text: I have been using Java since version 1.4. Both the language and its ecosystem have come a long way since then. I endured the height of the EJB phase. I adopted Spring when version 1.2 was released. I spent hours fighting with IDEs to run OSGi bundles. I hated building UIs with Swing/AWT, many of which are still in use today and are gradually being replaced by lovely JavaFX. When I look at code I wrote around 12 years ago, I'm amazed at how much I've matured too.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a personal historical perspective on Java's ecosystem challenges and improvements, which aligns with common community experiences.

comments.author: freedomben
comments.text: As someone who has been out of the Java world for many years, but recently forced back into it due to Android dev requirements, Post lawsuit, what is the relationship between Android (Google) and Java now?  When can we expect 26 on Android?  On that note, why is Android always so far behind?  Is it because Kotlin is primary or is it deeper?  Did the lawsuit play a role?
comments.analysis: The comment raises valid questions about Android's Java support and the impact of legal and ecosystem factors, reflecting common concerns but without definitive answers.

comments.author: dzonga
comments.text:
the people that work on Java & the JVM are very smart.

it has become a best of breed language - hell its better than Go for industry purposes.

the drawback with Java will always be the CULTURE - (maybe someone can insert a quote of how in physics progress is only made, when old physicist die - I don't wanna be morbid ) but with Java same that's when the culture will change.

All those people using typescript (could be using Java - but the culture doesn't want them and consider them heretics for not embracing religion of OOP and FactoryFactory)
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully addresses cultural resistance in the Java community, though the comparison to Go and cultural claims are somewhat subjective.

comments.author: wiseowise
comments.text: Android as always in shambles. Shame that neither Google, nor ecosystem cares about Java because they’ve bought JetBrains kopium.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses frustration with Android's Java support and suggests a bias towards Kotlin/JetBrains, but it is somewhat speculative and emotionally charged.

comments.author: oystersareyum
comments.text: Do changes to Java itself impact e.g. Clojure? I think it uses many java primitives instead of emitting jvm byte code but I can be wrong.
comments.analysis: The comment raises a reasonable question about Java's impact on JVM languages like Clojure, though the technical details are slightly inaccurate.

comments.author: OSaMaBiNLoGiN
comments.text:
All the changes look great. But I don't know how I feel about the syntax. A lot of things that very well could be first-class just aren't. Instead of a `lazy` keyword, we get `LazyConstant<T>`.

I'm sure there's reasons as to why. I just don't know them.
comments.analysis: This comment thoughtfully critiques Java's syntax design decisions, reflecting common developer concerns about verbosity and lack of language-level features.

comments.author: jesse_dot_id
comments.text: Cool. I feel as though I'll never be able to escape 8, or ignore Ellison's legacy, unfortunately.
comments.analysis: The comment expresses a common sentiment about Java 8's long-lasting influence and legacy issues, though it lacks detailed explanation.
